title.zh: 卷六十五 皇甫張段列傳
title.en: Volume 65: Biographies of Huangfu, Zhang, Duan
title.raw: 卷六十五 皇甫張段列傳 Volume 65: Biographies of Huangfu, Zhang, Duan

sentences.zh: 皇甫規

translations.literal: Huangfu Gui.
translations.idiomatic: Huangfu Gui

sentences.zh: 皇甫規字威明，安定朝那人也。

translations.literal: Huangfu Gui, courtesy name Weiming, was a man of Chaona in Anding.
translations.idiomatic: Huangfu Gui, whose courtesy name was Weiming, came from Chaona in Anding commandery.

sentences.zh: 祖父稜，度遼將軍。

translations.literal: His grandfather Ling was General Who Crosses the Liao.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Huangfu Ling had served as General Who Crosses the Liao.

sentences.zh: 父旗，扶風都尉。

translations.literal: His father Qi was commandant of Fufeng.
translations.idiomatic: His father Huangfu Qi was commandant of Fufeng.

sentences.zh: 永和六年，西羌大寇三輔，圍安定，征西將軍馬賢將諸郡兵擊之，不能克。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Yonghe, the Western Qiang raided the Three Adjuncts on a large scale, besieged Anding, and General Who Conquers the West Ma Xian led the troops of the various commanderies against them but could not overcome them.
translations.idiomatic: In Yonghe 6 (141 CE), the Western Qiang poured into the Three Adjuncts and surrounded Anding. Ma Xian, the general charged with campaigning westward, threw the united forces of several commanderies against them but failed to break the siege.

sentences.zh: 規雖在布衣，見賢不恤軍事，審其必敗，乃上書言狀。

translations.literal: Although Gui was in commoner clothes, he saw that Xian did not attend to military affairs, discerned that he would certainly be defeated, and therefore submitted a memorial stating the situation.
translations.idiomatic: Gui was still a private scholar, yet he could see that Ma Xian was neglecting his command; convinced the campaign was doomed, he memorialized the throne and laid out the facts.

sentences.zh: 尋而賢果為羌所沒。

translations.literal: Soon afterward Xian indeed was destroyed by the Qiang.
translations.idiomatic: Before long Ma Xian was cut off and overrun by the Qiang, just as Gui had foreseen.

sentences.zh: 郡將知規有兵略，乃命為功曹，使率甲士八百，與羌交戰，斬首數級，賊遂退卻。

translations.literal: The commandery commander knew Gui had military stratagems, therefore appointed him merit clerk, had him lead eight hundred armored soldiers, engage the Qiang in battle, behead several ranks, and the bandits then withdrew.
translations.idiomatic: The commandery commander, recognizing Gui’s grasp of strategy, made him merit clerk and gave him eight hundred armored troops; they clashed with the Qiang, took several heads, and the raiders fell back.

sentences.zh: 舉規上計掾。

translations.literal: Gui was recommended as clerk for the annual accounting.
translations.idiomatic: He was then nominated as clerk to accompany the commandery’s accounts to the capital.

sentences.zh: 其後羌觿大合，攻燒隴西，朝廷患之。

translations.literal: Afterward the Qiang tribes assembled in great force, attacked and burned Longxi, and the court worried over it.
translations.idiomatic: Later the Qiang confederation massed, struck Longxi, and put towns to the torch, which alarmed the central government.

sentences.zh: 規乃上疏求乞自□，曰：「臣比年以來，數陳便宜。

translations.literal: Gui then submitted a memorial begging to offer himself (text damaged:), saying, "In recent years I have repeatedly presented advantageous proposals."
translations.idiomatic: Gui therefore memorialized, offering to take a commission himself, and wrote: "These past few years I have again and again urged policies that would serve the state."

sentences.zh: 羌戎未動，策其將反，馬賢始出，頗知必敗。

translations.literal: Before the Qiang tribes had moved, I predicted they would rebel; when Ma Xian first set out, I largely knew he would certainly be defeated.
translations.idiomatic: Before the Qiang had even stirred I warned they would rise; the moment Ma Xian marched, I could see his expedition would end in disaster.

sentences.zh: 誤中之言，在可考校。

translations.literal: Words that hit by mistake are subject to examination and verification.
translations.idiomatic: Whether my judgments were lucky guesses or sound forecasts is something the records can verify.

sentences.zh: 臣每惟賢等擁觿四年，未有成功，懸師之費且百億計，[1]出於平人，回入奸吏。

translations.literal: I constantly reflect that Xian and the others have held their armies four years without achieving success; the expense of expeditions far from home amounts to nearly ten billion cash, [1] coming from ordinary people and circling back into corrupt officials.
translations.idiomatic: I keep thinking how Ma Xian and his ilk have kept huge forces in the field for four years with nothing to show for it, while the cost of those stranded armies runs toward ten billion cash—money squeezed from common households and siphoned off by venal clerks.

sentences.zh: [2]故江湖之人，腢為盜賊，青、徐荒饑，襁負流散。

translations.literal: [2] Therefore people along the rivers and lakes have thereby turned into bandits; Qing and Xu suffer famine, and people flee carrying children on their backs.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Peasants along the great waterways have been driven to banditry; Qingzhou and Xuzhou are wasted by hunger, and families bundle their infants and scatter as refugees.

sentences.zh: 夫羌戎潰叛，不由承平，皆由邊將失於綏御。

translations.literal: As for the Qiang tribes breaking and rebelling, it is not because of times of peace; all is because frontier generals fail in soothing and controlling them.
translations.idiomatic: The Qiang did not rebel because the realm was at peace; they rose because frontier commanders mishandled conciliation and control.

sentences.zh: 乘常守安，則君侵暴，苟競小利，則致大害，微勝則虛張首級，軍敗則隱匿不言。

translations.literal: Riding on constant guard and peace, then rulers invade and brutalize; if they compete for petty profit, then they bring great harm; with slight victories they falsely inflate head counts, and when the army is defeated they conceal and do not speak.
translations.idiomatic: When local commanders enjoy calm posts they turn to oppression; when they chase petty gains they invite catastrophe. A minor success prompts them to pad kill counts, while a rout never reaches the emperor’s ears.

sentences.zh: 軍士勞怨，困於猾吏，進不得快戰以徼功，退不得溫飽以全命，餓死溝渠，暴骨中原。

translations.literal: Soldiers labor and resent, are trapped by crafty clerks, advancing cannot get swift battle to seek merit, retreating cannot get warmth and fullness to preserve life; they starve to death in ditches and expose their bones on the central plain.
translations.idiomatic: The troops are exhausted and bitter, at the mercy of corrupt handlers: they cannot advance to win glory in open battle, nor fall back to food and shelter enough to survive. They die of hunger in the hedgerows and leave their bones whitening on the Central Plain.

sentences.zh: 徒見王師之出，不聞振旅之聲。

translations.literal: They only see the royal army’s departure, and do not hear the sound of troops returning in good order.
translations.idiomatic: The people watch imperial columns march out year after year, yet never hear the drums of a victorious homecoming.

sentences.zh: [3]酋豪泣血，驚懼生變。

translations.literal: [3] Chieftains weep blood, startled and fearful, and changes arise.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Tribal headmen weep blood in terror, fearing they will be provoked into fresh revolt.

sentences.zh: 是以安不能久，敗則經年。

translations.literal: Therefore peace cannot last long, and when defeated it lasts whole years.
translations.idiomatic: Hence any calm is brief, while defeat drags on for years.

sentences.zh: 臣所以搏手叩心而增歎者也。

translations.literal: This is why I wring my hands, beat my breast, and increase my sighs.
translations.idiomatic: That is why I wring my hands and strike my breast in ever deeper grief.

sentences.zh: 願假臣兩營二郡，[4]屯列坐食之兵五千，出其不意，與護羌校尉趙沖共相首尾。

translations.literal: I beg to be lent two camps and two commanderies, [4] five thousand troops stationed in ranks who sit and eat, strike unexpectedly, and with Colonel Protector of the Qiang Zhao Chong mutually coordinate front and rear.
translations.idiomatic: I ask only for authority over two garrisons and two commanderies—five thousand idle camp troops—and permission to hit the enemy where they do not expect it, while Zhao Chong, the Colonel Protector of the Qiang, pins them from the other direction.

sentences.zh: 土地山谷，臣所曉習；

translations.literal: The land, earth, mountains, and valleys—I understand and am practiced in them;
translations.idiomatic: I know every ridge and valley of that country;

sentences.zh: 兵埶巧便，臣已更之。

translations.literal: the troops’ deployments’ skill and convenience—I have experienced them.
translations.idiomatic: I have repeatedly handled troops there and learned how to use the terrain.

sentences.zh: 可不煩方寸之印，尺帛之賜，高可以滌患，下可以納降。

translations.literal: One need not trouble with the seal of a square inch or the gift of a foot of silk; on high it can wash away calamity, below it can bring in surrender.
translations.idiomatic: The court need not lavish seals or silks on me: at best I will scour away the threat, at least I will win surrenders.

sentences.zh: 若謂臣年少官輕，不足用者，凡諸敗將，非官爵之不高，年齒之不邁。

translations.literal: If it is said that I am young in years and light in office and not sufficient to employ—as for all those defeated generals, it was not that their ranks were not high or their ages not advanced.
translations.idiomatic: If youth and low rank disqualify me, recall how many beaten commanders held lofty titles and grizzled heads.

sentences.zh: [5]臣不勝至誠，沒死自陳。

translations.literal: [5] Your servant cannot overcome utmost sincerity; drowning in death I state myself.
translations.idiomatic: [5] I speak with all the candor I can muster, though it cost me my life.

sentences.zh: 」時帝不能用。

translations.literal: When he closed the memorial, the emperor did not adopt his proposal.
translations.idiomatic: The throne took no notice of his advice.

sentences.zh: 沖質之閒，梁太后臨朝，規舉賢良方正。

translations.literal: Between Emperors Chong and Zhi, Empress Dowager Liang faced the court, and Gui was recommended as worthy, good, and upright.
translations.idiomatic: During the brief reigns of the boy emperors Chong and Zhi, Empress Dowager Liang held the regency, and Gui was nominated as a candidate in the worthy-and-upright examination.

sentences.zh: 對策曰：

translations.literal: He replied to the examination question, saying:
translations.idiomatic: His examination essay ran:

sentences.zh: 伏惟孝順皇帝，初勤王政，紀綱四方，幾以獲安。

translations.literal: I humbly consider that Emperor Xiaoshun, at first diligently administered royal government, ordered the four quarters, and nearly thereby obtained peace.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Shun began his reign with earnest attention to governance; he tightened discipline across the empire and the realm nearly knew calm.

sentences.zh: 後遭奸偽，威分近習，[6]畜貨聚馬，戲謔是聞；

translations.literal: Afterward he encountered treacherous pretense, authority divided among those close and accustomed, [6] hoarding goods and gathering horses, jesting and banter were what was heard;
translations.idiomatic: Then deceitful favorites clawed away his authority; [6] the palace hoarded wealth and horses while rumor spoke only of sport and ridicule;

sentences.zh: 又因緣嬖倖，受賂賣爵，輕使賓客，交錯其閒，天下擾擾，從亂如歸，[7]故每有征戰，鮮不挫傷，官民並竭，上下窮虛。

translations.literal: also relying on favored attendants, accepting bribes and selling ranks, lightly dispatching retainers, crisscrossing among them; the realm was riotous, following disorder as if returning home, [7] therefore whenever there were campaigns, rarely were they not thwarted; officials and people together exhausted, above and below impoverished and empty.
translations.idiomatic: pet favorites sold offices for silver and sent their hangers-on to weave a web of intrigue. The empire seethed; men drifted into rebellion as naturally as homing birds. [7] Every campaign ended in bloody setback; treasury and populace were bled white from court to countryside.

sentences.zh: 臣在關西，竊聽風聲，未聞國家有所先後，[8]而威福之來，咸歸權幸。

translations.literal: Your servant is west of the passes and secretly listens to reports; I have not heard the state have any order of priority, [8] but as for the coming of awesome fortune, all returns to powerful favorites.
translations.idiomatic: From west of the passes I catch only whispers of policy; [8] I never hear the court set clear priorities—only that favour and fear alike flow from the men around the throne.

sentences.zh: 陛下體兼乾坤，聰哲純茂。

translations.literal: Your Majesty’s body combines Qian and Kun, intelligent and wise, pure and abundant.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty unites heaven and earth in one person; your discernment is luminous and whole.

sentences.zh: 攝政之初，拔用忠貞，其餘維綱，多所改正。

translations.literal: At the beginning of holding the government, you elevated and employed the loyal and steadfast; as for the remaining sustaining cords, many were corrected.
translations.idiomatic: When you first took the regency you promoted men of proven loyalty and reworked the instruments of rule.

sentences.zh: 遠近翕然，望見太平。

translations.literal: Far and near were unified, hoping to see great peace.
translations.idiomatic: Near and far breathed easier, daring to hope for an age of peace.

sentences.zh: 而地震之後，霧氣白濁，日月不光，旱魃為虐，[9]大賊從橫，流血丹野，庶品不安，譴誠累至，殆以奸臣權重之所致也。

translations.literal: But after the earthquake, mist and vapor were white and turbid, sun and moon lacked brightness, the drought demon acted cruelly, [9] great bandits ran rampant, blood flowed reddening the wilds, the myriad kinds were not secure, reproofs and warnings arrived repeatedly—almost because treacherous ministers wielded excessive power.
translations.idiomatic: Yet since the earth shook, milky haze has veiled the sky, the lights of sun and moon have dimmed, drought fiends have scourged the fields, [9] and great rebels course unchecked—blood has dyed the countryside crimson. Every living order trembles under repeated heaven-sent warnings, surely because vicious ministers wield too much power.

sentences.zh: 其常侍尤無狀者，亟便黜遣，[10]披埽凶黨，收入財賄，以塞痛怨，以荅天誡。

translations.literal: As for those attendants at court especially without decent bearing, urgently dismiss and send them away, [10] sweep away the vicious clique, confiscate their wealth and bribes, in order to stop bitter resentment and in order to answer heaven’s warning.
translations.idiomatic: The most outrageous of the palace attendants should be cashiered at once; [10] rip out their criminal faction, seize their loot, quiet the people’s fury, and answer the reproof from above.

sentences.zh: 今大將軍梁冀、河南尹不疑，處周、邵之任，為社稷之鎮，加與王室世為姻族，[11]今日立號雖尊可也，[12]實宜增修謙節，輔以儒術，省去游娛不急之務，割減廬第無益之飾。

translations.literal: Now Grand General Liang Ji and Governor of Henan Buyi occupy the offices of the Zhou and Shao, act as the pillar of altars of soil and grain, moreover with the royal house are generation after generation marriage kin; [11] today establishing titles although honorable is acceptable, [12] truly they ought to increase and cultivate humble restraint, assist with Confucian arts, cut away roaming amusement and non-urgent affairs, reduce and cut mansions’ useless ornament.
translations.idiomatic: Your Grand General Liang Ji and Governor of Henan Liang Buyi stand in the places once held by the Duke of Zhou and the Duke of Shao; they anchor the dynasty and are tied to the imperial clan by marriage after marriage. [11] Their honors are deservedly high; [12] still they should deepen their modesty, ground themselves in Confucian teaching, shed idle pleasures and needless display, and strip their mansions of wasteful finery.

sentences.zh: 夫君者舟也，人者水也。

translations.literal: The ruler is the boat; the people are the water.
translations.idiomatic: The sovereign is a boat; the common people are the water that bears or swamps it.

sentences.zh: [13]腢臣乘舟者也，將軍兄弟操鐐者也。

translations.literal: [13] Your ministers are those who ride the boat; the general and his brothers are those who hold the oars.
translations.idiomatic: [13] Your officials ride in that vessel; General Liang and his brothers grip the helm.

sentences.zh: 若能平志畢力，以度元元，所謂福也。

translations.literal: If able to level the will and exhaust strength, in order to deliver the multitude, this is called fortune.
translations.idiomatic: If they steady their hearts and bend every effort to carry the people through, that is true good fortune.

sentences.zh: 如其怠□，將淪波濤。

translations.literal: If they are negligent (text damaged:), they will sink into waves and billows.
translations.idiomatic: Should they slacken or grow careless, ship and crew alike will vanish under the swell.

sentences.zh: 可不慎乎！

translations.literal: Can one not be cautious!
translations.idiomatic: Can they afford anything less than the utmost care?

sentences.zh: 夫德不稱祿，猶鑿墉之趾，以益其高。

translations.literal: As for virtue not matching emolument, it is like chiseling the base of a wall in order to increase its height.
translations.idiomatic: Virtue that does not match one’s salary is like undermining a wall’s foundation to make the parapet taller.

sentences.zh: 豈量力審功安固之道哉？

translations.literal: Is this the way of measuring strength and examining merit for security and solidity?
translations.idiomatic: Surely that is no way to weigh one’s strength, judge real achievement, or build something that will stand.

sentences.zh: 凡諸宿猾、酒徒、戲客，皆耳納邪聲，口出諂言，甘心逸游，唱造不義。

translations.literal: As for all longstanding crafty men, wine fellows, and jesting guests, all receive evil sounds in the ear, utter flattery from the mouth, willingly indulge in wandering play, and advocate creating unrighteousness.
translations.idiomatic: The hardened intriguers, drunkards, and hangers-on who throng your gates soak up vicious gossip, speak only flattery, live for pleasure, and urge unjust deeds.

sentences.zh: 亦宜貶斥，以懲不軌。

translations.literal: Also ought to be demoted and expelled, in order to punish deviation from the norm.
translations.idiomatic: Cast them out as a warning against misconduct.

sentences.zh: 令冀等深思得賢之福，失人之累。

translations.literal: Make Ji and the others deeply ponder the fortune of obtaining the worthy and the burden of losing people.
translations.idiomatic: Bid Liang Ji reflect on the blessing of wise ministers and the ruin that follows when talent is driven away.

sentences.zh: 又在位素餐，尚書怠職，有司依違，莫肯糾察，故使陛下專受諂諛之言，不聞戶牖之外。

translations.literal: Moreover those in office eat salary without work, the Masters of Writing neglect duty, responsible offices hesitate and waver, none willing to investigate and inspect, therefore causing Your Majesty solely to receive flattery and sycophancy, and not hear beyond doors and windows.
translations.idiomatic: Officeholders feast on state grain while doing nothing; the Masters of Writing shirk their briefs; yamen clerks shuffle and dodge—none will call corruption by its name. That is why Your Majesty hears only adulation and nothing of the world beyond the palace shutters.

sentences.zh: 臣誠知阿諛有福，深言近禍，豈敢隱心以避誅責乎！

translations.literal: Your servant truly knows flattery has fortune and deep speech draws near calamity—how dare I hide the heart to avoid blame and punishment!
translations.idiomatic: I know frank speech courts disaster while smooth words win ease—yet I will not smother what I believe to escape punishment.

sentences.zh: 臣生長邊遠，希涉紫庭，怖懾失守，言不盡心。

translations.literal: Your servant was born and grew on the distant frontier, rarely treading the purple court, fearful and trembling losing control, words do not exhaust the heart.
translations.idiomatic: Raised on the outer marches, seldom admitted to this purple hall, I tremble so that my words may fail to voice the fullness of my mind.

sentences.zh: 梁冀忿其刺己，以規為下第，拜郎中。

translations.literal: Liang Ji resented that Gui had criticized him, ranked Gui at the bottom of the list, and appointed him gentleman consultant.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji, smarting at Gui’s barbs, ranked him last among the candidates and gave him a sinceure as gentleman consultant.

sentences.zh: 託疾免歸，州郡承冀旨，幾陷死者再三。

translations.literal: He claimed illness and was dismissed returning home; commanderies and prefectures received Ji’s intent and several times nearly trapped him to death.
translations.idiomatic: Gui pleaded illness and went home, but local authorities, doing Liang Ji’s bidding, thrice nearly engineered his death.

sentences.zh: 遂以詩、易教授，門徒三百餘人，積十四年。

translations.literal: Thereupon with the Odes and Changes he taught, disciples more than three hundred, accumulating fourteen years.
translations.idiomatic: He turned to teaching the Classic of Poetry and the Book of Changes to over three hundred pupils and kept at it for fourteen years.

sentences.zh: 後梁冀被誅，旬月之閒，禮命五至，皆不就。

translations.literal: Afterward Liang Ji was executed; within ten days to a month, ceremonial appointments arrived five times; all he did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: After Liang Ji’s execution the court summoned him five times within a month; he refused every offer.

sentences.zh: 時太山賊叔孫無忌侵亂郡縣，中郎將宗資討之未服。

translations.literal: At that time Mount Tai bandit Sun Wuji invaded and threw into disorder commanderies and counties; General of the Household Zong Zi attacked them but they did not submit.
translations.idiomatic: Bandits under Sun Wuji were ravaging Taishan commandery, and Zong Zi, the general of the household sent against them, could not bring them to heel.

sentences.zh: 公車特徵規，拜太山太守。

translations.literal: The public carriage specially summoned Gui, appointed governor of Mount Tai.
translations.idiomatic: An imperial coach was dispatched to fetch Gui, and he was named governor of Taishan.

sentences.zh: 規到官，廣設方略，寇賊悉平。

translations.literal: When Gui arrived at office he broadly set stratagems; bandits all were pacified.
translations.idiomatic: Once in office he deployed a wide range of measures and soon stamped out the insurgents.

sentences.zh: 延熹四年秋，叛羌零吾等與先零別種寇鈔關中，護羌校尉段熲坐征。

translations.literal: In autumn of the fourth year of Yanxi, rebel Qiang Lingwu and others with separate tribes of the Xianling raided Guanzhong; Colonel Protector of the Qiang Duan Jiong was impeached and recalled.
translations.idiomatic: In the autumn of Yanxi 4 (161 CE), Lingwu and other rebel Qiang bands—kinsmen of the Xianling—pillaged Guanzhong, and Duan Jiong, the Colonel Protector of the Qiang, was impeached and recalled.

sentences.zh: [14]後先零諸種陸梁，覆沒營塢。

translations.literal: [14] Afterward Xianling various kinds were rampant, overturned and drowned camps and forts.
translations.idiomatic: [14] The Xianling confederation then rose in force and overran the frontier camps.

sentences.zh: [15]規素悉羌事，志自奮□，乃上疏曰：「自臣受任，志竭愚鈍，實賴兗州刺史牽顥之清猛，中郎將宗資之信義，得承節度，幸無咎譽。

translations.literal: [15] Gui had long been thoroughly versed in Qiang affairs and was resolved to devote himself (text damaged:). He submitted a memorial: "Since I took this post I have given my dim best; I owe what success I have to the stern integrity of Yanzhou Inspector Qian Hao and the good faith of General of the Household Zong Zi, who let me follow their plans and kept me from disgrace."
translations.idiomatic: [15] Gui knew the Qiang situation intimately and burned to offer himself; he memorialized: "Since taking office I have strained my meager talents, indebted above all to Inspector Qian Hao of Yanzhou for his integrity and bite and to General Zong Zi for reliable support—only thus could I follow orders and escape censure."

sentences.zh: 今猾賊就滅，太山略平，復聞腢羌並皆反逆。

translations.literal: Now crafty bandits are on verge of extinction, Mount Tai is roughly pacified; again I hear the various Qiang together all rebel.
translations.idiomatic: The Taishan bandits are nearly gone and the commandery is quiet again, yet word comes that the Qiang along the frontier have risen in concert.

sentences.zh: 臣生長邠岐，年五十有九，昔為郡吏，再更叛羌，豫籌其事，有誤中之言。

translations.literal: Your servant was born and grew in Bin and Qi, age fifty-nine, formerly was commandery clerk, twice experienced rebel Qiang, pre-planned these affairs, had words that hit by mistake.
translations.idiomatic: I am a man of Bin and Qi, fifty-nine years old, once a commandery clerk who lived through two Qiang revolts and warned of them beforehand—some of those warnings proved uncannily accurate.

sentences.zh: 臣素有固疾，恐犬馬齒窮，不報大恩，願乞冗官，備單車一介之使，勞來三輔，宣國威澤，以所習地形兵埶，佐助諸軍。

translations.literal: Your servant has long suffered chronic illness and fears my allotted span will end before I repay your grace; I beg a minor redundant post—as envoy with but one cart—to console and reassure the Three Adjuncts, spread imperial majesty and bounty, and lend the armies the terrain and tactics I know.
translations.idiomatic: Chronic illness gnaws at me; I fear my years will run out before I repay your kindness. Grant me even a minor posting—a lone envoy with a single cart—to rally the Three Adjuncts, proclaim imperial majesty, and put my knowledge of the terrain and tactics at the army’s disposal.

sentences.zh: 臣窮居孤危之中，坐觀郡將，已數十年矣。

translations.literal: Your servant in poverty dwells in solitary peril, sitting and observing commandery generals, already several tens of years.
translations.idiomatic: For decades I have watched those commandery generals from the margins, poor and exposed.

sentences.zh: 自鳥鼠至於東岱，其病一也。

translations.literal: From Niaoshu east to Eastern Dai, their illness is one.
translations.idiomatic: From the Niaoshu Mountains to Mount Tai the sickness is the same: misrule on the frontier.

sentences.zh: [16]力求猛敵，不如清平；

translations.literal: [16] Striving to seek fierce enemies is not as good as purity and peace;
translations.idiomatic: [16] Seeking ever fiercer foes avails less than honest, competent government;

sentences.zh: 勤明吳、孫，未若奉法。

translations.literal: diligently clarifying Wu and Sun is not like obeying law.
translations.idiomatic: clever stratagems like Wu Qi’s or Sun Wu’s mean nothing next to faithful adherence to the law.

sentences.zh: [17]前變未遠，臣誠戚之。

translations.literal: [17] The previous change is not far; your servant truly grieves for it.
translations.idiomatic: [17] The last uprising is still a fresh wound; it fills me with dread.

sentences.zh: [18]是以越職，盡其區區。」

translations.literal: [18] Therefore crossing office, exhausting my petty sincerity."
translations.idiomatic: [18] So I overstep my rank to speak this humble plea."

sentences.zh: 至冬，羌遂大合，朝廷為憂。

translations.literal: When winter came the Qiang then united in great force, and the court was troubled.
translations.idiomatic: By winter the Qiang had massed in overwhelming strength, and Luoyang grew alarmed.

sentences.zh: 三公舉規為中郎將，持節監關西兵，討零吾等，破之，斬首八百級。

translations.literal: The Three Excellencies recommended Gui as general of the household, bearing credentials supervising troops west of the passes, attacking Lingwu and others, defeating them, beheading eight hundred ranks.
translations.idiomatic: The Three Excellencies nominated Gui as general of the household with imperial insignia to oversee the armies west of the passes; he smashed Lingwu’s host and took eight hundred heads.

sentences.zh: 先零諸種羌慕規威信，相勸降者十餘萬。

translations.literal: Xianling various-kind Qiang admired Gui’s prestige and trust; mutually persuaded to surrender were more than one hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: Impressed by Gui’s authority and good faith, more than a hundred thousand Xianling Qiang let themselves be talked into submission.

sentences.zh: 明年，規因發其騎共討隴右，而道路隔絕，軍中大疫，死者十三四。

translations.literal: The next year Gui therefore mobilized his cavalry jointly to attack Longyou, but roads were cut off, in the army a great plague, dead three or four in ten.
translations.idiomatic: The following year he led his horsemen into Longyou, only to find the roads blocked and plague raging through the camps—three or four men in ten died.

sentences.zh: 規親入庵廬，巡視將士，三軍感悅。

translations.literal: Gui personally entered the tents, inspected officers and soldiers; the three armies were moved and pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Gui walked the sick tents himself, steadying his officers and men, and the whole army rallied.

sentences.zh: 東羌遂遣使乞降，涼州復通。

translations.literal: The Eastern Qiang thereupon sent envoys begging to surrender; Liangzhou was again open.
translations.idiomatic: The eastern Qiang sued for peace, and traffic with Liangzhou reopened.

sentences.zh: 先是安定太守孫鑈受取狼籍，屬國都尉李翕、督軍御史張稟多殺降羌，涼州刺史郭閎、漢陽太守趙熹並老弱不堪任職，而皆倚恃權貴，不遵法度。

translations.literal: Earlier Anding Governor Sun Ao took bribes in disorderly fashion; Dependent State Commandant Li Xi and Army Supervising Secretary Zhang Bing killed many surrendered Qiang in excess; Liangzhou Inspector Guo Hong and Hanyang Governor Zhao Xi together were old and weak and could not bear holding office, yet all relied on powerful nobles and did not observe laws and regulations.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier the governor of Anding, Sun Ao, had lined his pockets shamelessly; Li Xi, commandant of the dependent state, and Zhang Bing, the army secretary, had butchered surrendered Qiang wholesale; Guo Hong, inspector of Liangzhou, and Zhao Xi, governor of Hanyang, were aged incompetents propped up by patrons who flouted every statute.

sentences.zh: 規到州界，悉條奏其罪，或免或誅。

translations.literal: When Gui reached the province boundary he fully itemized and memorialized their crimes; some were dismissed, some executed.
translations.idiomatic: As soon as Gui crossed into the province he memorialized each misdeed; some officials were cashiered, others executed.

sentences.zh: 羌人聞之，翕然反善。

translations.literal: When the Qiang heard it they unifiedly returned to goodness.
translations.idiomatic: The Qiang heard the news and willingly returned to allegiance.

sentences.zh: 沉氐大豪滇昌、饑恬等十餘萬口，復詣規降。

translations.literal: Chenshi great chiefs Dianchang, Jitian, and others more than one hundred thousand mouths again went to Gui to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: More than a hundred thousand Chenshi tribesmen led by headmen such as Dianchang and Jitian came again to surrender to Gui.

sentences.zh: 規出身數年，持節為將，擁觿立功，還督鄉里，既無它私惠，而多所舉奏，又惡絕宦官，不與交通，於是中外並怨，遂共誣規貨賂腢羌，令其文降。

translations.literal: Gui had emerged from office several years, bearing credentials as general, embracing armies and establishing merit, returned supervising his home village; having no other private favors yet many impeachments, also loathed cutting off eunuchs and did not communicate with them; therefore inside and outside together resented him, then jointly slandered Gui bribing the various Qiang, making them feigned surrender.
translations.idiomatic: Gui had spent years in the field with imperial seal in hand, commanding large forces and winning victories, then returned to oversee his native commandery. He granted no private favors but impeached widely, despised the eunuchs, and refused to deal with them—so court and countryside alike nursed grudges and jointly accused him of buying sham surrenders from the Qiang with public funds.

sentences.zh: [19]天子璽書誚讓相屬。

translations.literal: [19] The Son of Heaven’s sealed letters of reproach followed one another.
translations.idiomatic: [19] Imperial edicts bristling with reproof arrived one after another.

sentences.zh: 規懼不免，上疏自訟曰：「四年之秋，戎丑蠢戾，[20]爰自西州，侵及涇陽，[21]舊都懼駭，朝廷西顧。

translations.literal: Gui feared he could not escape and submitted a memorial defending himself, saying: "In the autumn of the fourth year [Yanxi], the western barbarians rose in brutal rebellion, [20] spreading from Liangzhou toward Jingyang, [21] striking terror into the old capital and forcing the court to look anxiously westward.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing he could not clear his name, Gui memorialized in his own defense: "In the autumn of my fourth year in office the western tribes rose in violence; [20] rebellion rolled out of Liangzhou toward Jingyang, [21] terrifying the old capital and forcing the court to anxiously scan the western horizon."

sentences.zh: 明詔不以臣愚駑，急使軍就道。

translations.literal: The enlightened edict did not regard your servant foolish and dull, urgently ordered the army on the road.
translations.idiomatic: Your enlightened decree overlooked my mediocrity and ordered my troops forward without delay.

sentences.zh: [22]幸蒙威靈，遂振國命，羌戎諸種，大小稽首，輒移書營郡，以訪誅納，[23]所省之費，一億以上。

translations.literal: [22] By fortune we borrowed your awesome presence and revived the state’s authority; Qiang and Rong of every stripe, great and small, knocked their heads in submission. Orders passed constantly between camps and commanderies on whom to strike and whom to accept; [23] funds spared totaled upwards of one hundred million.
translations.idiomatic: [22] Under your majesty’s awe I restored imperial writ: every Qiang clan, great or small, submitted. Orders went to camps and commanderies on whom to execute and whom to enroll; [23] the treasury saved upwards of one hundred million cash.

sentences.zh: 以為忠臣之義，不敢告勞，[24]故恥以片言自及微□。

translations.literal: Considering a loyal minister’s duty not to boast of hardship, [24] I was ashamed to trouble you with petty self-reference (text damaged:).
translations.idiomatic: A loyal servant does not boast of his toil; [24] I was ashamed to trouble you with petty self-praise.

sentences.zh: 然比方先事，庶免罪悔。

translations.literal: Yet comparing to prior affairs, perhaps avoided crime and regret.
translations.idiomatic: Still, set beside earlier disasters, I hoped I had earned reprieve rather than blame.

sentences.zh: [25]前踐州界，先奏郡守孫鑈，次及屬國都尉李翕、督軍御史張稟；

translations.literal: [25] Previously treading the province boundary, first memorialized Governor Sun Ao, next reached Dependent State Commandant Li Xi, Army Supervising Secretary Zhang Bing;
translations.idiomatic: [25] On entering the province I impeached Governor Sun Ao first, then Commandant Li Xi and Secretary Zhang Bing;

sentences.zh: 旋師南征，又上涼州刺史郭閎、漢陽太守趙熹，陳其過惡，執據大辟。

translations.literal: Turning army south campaigning, again submitted Liangzhou Inspector Guo Hong, Hanyang Governor Zhao Xi, stating their excesses and evil, grasping evidence of capital crime.
translations.idiomatic: When I swung south I charged Inspector Guo Hong and Governor Zhao Xi with crimes warranting execution and backed each count with proof.

sentences.zh: 凡此五臣，支黨半國，其餘墨綬，下至小吏，所連及者，復有百餘。

translations.literal: All these five ministers, branches and factions half the state; the remaining black-ribbon ones down to petty clerks, those connected and reaching, again more than one hundred.
translations.idiomatic: Those five men commanded factions that sprawled across half the bureaucracy; black-girdled officials and petty clerks caught in their net numbered another hundred-plus.

sentences.zh: 吏托報將之怨，子思復父之恥，載贄馳車，懷糧步走，交構豪門，競流謗讟，雲臣私報諸羌，謝其錢貨。

translations.literal: Clerks relied on repaying generals’ resentment; sons thought of avenging fathers’ shame; carrying gifts riding swift carts, embracing grain walking quickly, intersecting and framing powerful gates, competing spreading slander and defamation, saying your servant privately repaid the various Qiang, thanking their money and goods.
translations.idiomatic: Their underlings sought revenge for dismissed patrons; sons hungered to wipe out a father’s disgrace. Bearing bribes, they raced by carriage or foot to powerful houses and poured out slander that I had secretly paid off the Qiang with treasure.

sentences.zh: [26]若臣以私財，則家無擔石；

translations.literal: [26] If your servant used private wealth, then the household has no picul or stone;
translations.idiomatic: [26] Had I used private means, you would find not a bushel of grain in my house;

sentences.zh: 如物出於官，則文簿易考。

translations.literal: if goods came out from official sources, then documents and registers are easy to examine.
translations.idiomatic: if state funds were involved, the ledgers would give easy proof.

sentences.zh: 就臣愚惑，信如言者，前世尚遺匈奴以宮姬，[27]鎮烏孫以公主。

translations.literal: Approaching your servant’s foolish confusion, if truly as the words say, former ages still presented palace beauties to the Xiongnu, [27] stabilized Wusun with an imperial princess.
translations.idiomatic: Even accepting their absurd tale, former dynasties sent palace ladies to the Xiongnu [27] and married princesses to Wusun to keep the peace.

sentences.zh: [28]今臣但費千萬，以懷叛羌。

translations.literal: [28] Now your servant only spent ten million to soothe rebel Qiang.
translations.idiomatic: [28] I spent perhaps ten million cash to win over hostile tribes.

sentences.zh: 則良臣之才略，兵家之所貴，將有何罪，負義違理乎？

translations.literal: Then a good minister’s talent and strategy, what the military family esteems—what crime would there be, betraying righteousness and violating principle?
translations.idiomatic: If that is the mark of a capable minister—the very quality armies prize—what offense have I committed against justice?

sentences.zh: 自永初以來，將出不少，覆軍有五，動資巨億。

translations.literal: Since Yongchu, generals going out have not been few; armies overturned have been five; movements costing vast billions.
translations.idiomatic: Since the Yongchu era generals have marched in endless succession; five armies have been annihilated and each campaign drains billions from the treasury.

sentences.zh: 有旋車完封，寫之權門，[29]而名成功立，厚加爵封。

translations.literal: Some have turned their wagons with seals still intact and delivered the goods to great houses; [29] their reputations are made, their work praised, and they are showered with noble titles.
translations.idiomatic: Some run straight to the mighty with booty still under seal; [29] they win fame, collect rewards, and walk off with new titles and fiefs.

sentences.zh: 今臣還督本土，愨舉諸郡，絕交離親，戮辱舊故，觿謗陰害，固其宜也。

translations.literal: Now your servant has returned to oversee my home commandery, honestly impeaching one commandery after another, severing ties and alienating kin, shaming and striking down old associates—mass slander and secret attacks are surely to be expected.
translations.idiomatic: I came home to clean my own courtyard: I impeached corrupt governors, cut ties with patrons and kin alike, and exposed old cronies—small wonder their factions slander me and strike from the shadows.

sentences.zh: 臣雖污穢，廉絜無聞，今見覆沒，恥痛實深。

translations.literal: Your servant although filthy, unstained integrity unheard of, now seeing overturned and drowned, shame and pain are truly deep.
translations.idiomatic: I make no claim to purity, yet to be drowned in false accusation humiliates me beyond words.

sentences.zh: 傳稱『鹿死不擇音』，謹冒昧略上。

translations.literal: The tradition states ‘when the deer dies it does not choose its cry’; respectfully I rashly briefly submit.
translations.idiomatic: The texts say a hunted deer will bleat whatever sound may save it—I risk this blunt plea just so.

translations.literal: Closing quotation mark of Gui’s memorial.
translations.idiomatic: Here ends the passage quoted from Gui’s memorial.

sentences.zh: 其年冬，徵還拜議郎。

translations.literal: That winter he was summoned back and appointed gentleman consultant.
translations.idiomatic: Late that same winter an edict recalled him to court as gentleman consultant.

sentences.zh: 論功當封。

translations.literal: Discussing merit, he ought to have been enfeoffed.
translations.idiomatic: His victories plainly merited a noble fief.

sentences.zh: 而中常侍徐璜、左悺欲從求貨，數遣賓客就問功狀，規終不荅。

translations.literal: But Regular Palace Attendants Xu Huang and Zuo Guan wished to seek goods from him, repeatedly sent retainers to inquire about his merit report; Gui to the end did not answer.
translations.idiomatic: Attendants-in-ordinary Xu Huang and Zuo Guan demanded their share of the honors and kept dispatching clients to fish for details of his petition for rewards; Gui ignored every approach.

sentences.zh: 璜等忿怒，陷以前事，下之於吏。

translations.literal: Huang and the others were furious, framed him with prior affairs, handed him down to clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Enraged, they dredged up old charges and handed him over to the judiciary.

sentences.zh: 官屬欲賦斂請謝，規誓而不聽，遂以餘寇不絕，坐系廷尉，論輸左校。

translations.literal: His official subordinates wished to levy taxes to beg thanks; Gui swore and would not listen; thereupon because remaining bandits did not cease, he sat tied at the Commandant of Justice, sentenced to labor at the Left Workshops.
translations.idiomatic: His staff urged him to raise a levy and buy the eunuchs off; Gui swore he would not. They then blamed lingering banditry on him, had him thrown to the Commandant of Justice, and sentenced him to hard labor in the imperial workshops.

sentences.zh: [31]諸公及太學生張鳳等三百餘人詣闕訟之。

translations.literal: [31] Various dukes and Imperial University student Zhang Feng and others more than three hundred persons went to the palace gate to sue on his behalf.
translations.idiomatic: [31] More than three hundred high officials and academy scholars led by Zhang Feng marched to the palace gates to plead his case.

sentences.zh: 會赦，歸家。

translations.literal: Thereupon an amnesty; he returned home.
translations.idiomatic: A general amnesty followed and he went home.

sentences.zh: 徵拜度遼將軍，至營數月，上書薦中郎將張奐以自代。

translations.literal: Summoned and appointed General Who Crosses the Liao; arrived at camp several months, submitted memorial recommending General of the Household Zhang Huan to replace himself.
translations.idiomatic: Recalled and named General Who Crosses the Liao, he spent only a few months at headquarters before memorializing that General of the Household Zhang Huan should succeed him.

sentences.zh: 曰：「臣聞人無常俗，而政有治亂；

translations.literal: Saying: "Your servant has heard people have no constant customs, but government has order and chaos;
translations.idiomatic: He wrote: "I have heard that habits among the people shift, while rule may be sound or chaotic;

sentences.zh: 兵無強弱，而將有能否。

translations.literal: troops have no strong or weak, but generals have capable or incapable."
translations.idiomatic: troops are neither strong nor weak in themselves—it is commanders who prove able or inept."

sentences.zh: 伏見中郎將張奐，才略兼優，宜正元帥，以從觿望。

translations.literal: I humbly see General of the Household Zhang Huan, talent and strategy both excellent, ought to be appointed chief commander, in order to follow the armies’ expectation.
translations.idiomatic: General Zhang Huan combines counsel and courage; make him supreme commander and you will match what the host expects."

sentences.zh: 若猶謂愚臣宜充軍事者，願乞冗官，以為奐副。

translations.literal: If still saying foolish your servant ought to fill military affairs, I beg a supernumerary office to be Huan’s deputy.
translations.idiomatic: If Your Majesty still thinks this dull servant should serve in the field, grant me only a subordinate post under Zhang Huan."

sentences.zh: 」朝庭從之，以奐代為度遼將軍，規為使匈奴中郎將。

translations.literal: " The court followed it, made Huan replace as General Who Crosses the Liao, Gui as general of the household to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: " The court agreed: Zhang Huan became General Who Crosses the Liao; Gui was named general of the household for dealings with the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 及奐遷大司農，規復代為度遼將軍。

translations.literal: When Huan was transferred to grand minister of agriculture, Gui again replaced as General Who Crosses the Liao.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Huan rose to grand minister of agriculture, Gui once more took command as General Who Crosses the Liao.

sentences.zh: 規為人多意筭，自以連在大位，欲退身避第，數上病，不見聽。

translations.literal: Gui as a person had many schemes and calculations; himself considering continuous holding of high position, wished to withdraw body avoiding mansions, repeatedly submitted illness, was not heard.
translations.idiomatic: Gui was a subtle plotter; uneasy at holding one great post after another, he tried to step down and leave politics, pleading illness again and again without success.

sentences.zh: 會友人上郡太守王旻喪還，規縞素越界，到下亭迎之。

translations.literal: It happened a friend, Governor of Shangjun Wang Min’s funeral returned; Gui in mourning white crossed boundary, went to Lower Pavilion to welcome him.
translations.idiomatic: When his friend Wang Min, governor of Shangjun, was carried home for burial, Gui donned mourning white, crossed out of his jurisdiction, and met the cortège at Lower Pavilion.

sentences.zh: 因令客密告并州刺史胡芳，言規□遠軍營，公違禁憲，當急舉奏。

translations.literal: Thereupon ordered a client secretly to inform Bingzhou Inspector Hu Fang, saying Gui (text damaged:) far from army camp, openly violated prohibitions and statutes, ought urgently to memorialize.
translations.idiomatic: He then had a retainer tip off Bingzhou Inspector Hu Fang that Gui had abandoned his camp without leave—an open breach of statute fit for immediate impeachment.

sentences.zh: 芳曰：「威明欲避第仕塗，故激發我耳。

translations.literal: Fang said: "Weiming wants to slip away from high office and bait me into helping him."
translations.idiomatic: Hu Fang replied: "Weiming only wants an excuse to quit office—that is why he is baiting me."

sentences.zh: [32]吾當為朝廷愛才，何能申此子計邪！

translations.literal: [32] I ought for the court cherish talent, how can I extend this fellow’s scheme!
translations.idiomatic: [32] The throne needs men like him; I will not play along with this trick!"

sentences.zh: 」遂無所問。

translations.literal: " Thereupon nothing was investigated.
translations.idiomatic: " And he dropped the matter.

sentences.zh: 及黨事大起，天下名賢多見染逮，規雖為名將，素譽不高。

translations.literal: When the faction affair greatly arose, famous worthies of the realm mostly saw staining arrest; Gui although a famous general, plain reputation was not high.
translations.idiomatic: When the Great Proscription swept the empire, celebrated scholars fell into its net in droves; Gui, though a renowned commander, had never enjoyed lofty standing among them.

sentences.zh: 自以西州豪桀，恥不得豫，乃先自上言：「臣前薦故大司農張奐，是附黨也。

translations.literal: Considering himself a champion of the western marches, ashamed at being left off the lists, he memorialized first: "Your servant once recommended the former grand minister Zhang Huan—that makes me an ally of the faction.
translations.idiomatic: Counting himself a champion of the northwest, he was ashamed not to be listed and volunteered first: "I once recommended the former grand minister Zhang Huan—that makes me a partisan ally."

sentences.zh: 又臣昔論輸左校時，太學生張鳳等上書訟臣，是為黨人所附也。

translations.literal: Also when your servant was sentenced to the Left Workshops, Imperial University students led by Zhang Feng petitioned on my behalf—that shows partisans rallied to me.
translations.idiomatic: When I was sent to the Left Workshops, Zhang Feng and his fellow students petitioned on my behalf—that shows I was backed by the faction."

sentences.zh: 臣宜坐之。

translations.literal: Your servant ought to sit for it.
translations.idiomatic: Punish me with the rest."

sentences.zh: 」朝廷知而不問，時人以為規賢。

translations.literal: " The court knew but did not inquire; people of the time considered Gui worthy.
translations.idiomatic: " The court understood the gesture and took no action; contemporaries judged Gui the better man for it.

sentences.zh: 遷規弘農太守，封壽成亭侯，邑二百戶，讓封不受。

translations.literal: Gui was transferred Hongnong governor, enfeoffed Marquis of Shoucheng village, fief two hundred households; he declined the enfeoffment and did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted governor of Hongnong and offered the village marquisate of Shoucheng with two hundred households; he refused the fief.

sentences.zh: 再轉為護羌校尉。

translations.literal: Again transferred to Colonel Protector of the Qiang.
translations.idiomatic: He was soon reassigned as Colonel Protector of the Qiang.

sentences.zh: 熹平三年，以疾召還，未至，卒於谷城，年七十一。

translations.literal: In the third year of Xiping, because of illness summoned back; before arriving, died at Gucheng, age seventy-one.
translations.idiomatic: In Xiping 3 (174 CE) illness brought an edict recalling him; he died en route at Gucheng, aged seventy-one.

sentences.zh: 所著賦、銘、碑、贊、禱文、吊、章表、教令、書、檄、箋記，凡二十七篇。

translations.literal: What he authored—fu, ming, bei, zan, prayer texts, condolence pieces, memorials and petitions, instructions and orders, letters, proclamations, notes—in all twenty-seven pieces.
translations.idiomatic: His writings—rhapsodies, inscriptions, dirges, encomia, prayers, laments, memorials, edicts, letters, calls to arms, and private notes—total twenty-seven pieces.

sentences.zh: 論曰：孔子稱「其言之不怍，則其為之也難」。

translations.literal: The appraisal states: Confucius said "If in speaking one is not ashamed, then in acting it is difficult."
translations.idiomatic: The historian remarks: Confucius observed that when a man speaks without shame, carrying his words into deed becomes nearly impossible.

sentences.zh: [39]察皇甫規之言，其心不怍哉！

translations.literal: [39] Examine Huangfu Gui’s words—his heart was not ashamed!
translations.idiomatic: [39] Listen to Huangfu Gui’s memorials: here was a man who felt no shame about speaking the truth.

sentences.zh: 夫其審己則干祿，見賢則委位，故干祿不為貪，而委位不求讓；

translations.literal: As for him: when weighing his own case he sought office; when he saw worthier men he yielded post—thus seeking office was not greed, and yielding post sought no empty praise for modesty;
translations.idiomatic: When office suited him he took it; when someone better appeared he stepped aside—so his ambition never sank to greed, and his retirements carried no hunger for cheap praise;

sentences.zh: 稱己不疑伐，而讓人無懼情。

translations.literal: praising himself without suspecting self-praise, while yielding to others without fearful intent.
translations.idiomatic: he claimed merit without sounding vain and yielded rank without timidity.

sentences.zh: 故能功成於戎狄，身全於邦家也。

translations.literal: Therefore able to complete merit among Rong and Di, preserve body amid state and clan.
translations.idiomatic: Thus he won triumph on the barbarian frontier and kept his skin safe at court.

sentences.zh: 張奐字然明，敦煌*

translations.literal: Zhang Huan, courtesy name Ranming, Dunhuang*
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Huan, courtesy name Ranming, was a native of Dunhuang—

translations.literal: (wine)
translations.idiomatic: (scribal gloss in source)

sentences.zh: **〔淵〕*泉人也。

translations.literal: **〔Yuan〕*quan man (textual variant for the placename Yuanquan).
translations.idiomatic: —from Yuanquan in Dunhuang commandery (per textual note in the received text).

sentences.zh: [40]父惇，為漢陽太守。

translations.literal: [40] Father Dun was Hanyang governor.
translations.idiomatic: [40] His father Zhang Dun had served as governor of Hanyang.

sentences.zh: 奐少游三輔，師事太尉朱壟，學歐陽尚書。

translations.literal: Huan in youth traveled the Three Adjuncts, studied serving Grand Commandant Zhu Long, learned Ouyang Documents.
translations.idiomatic: In youth he studied in the capital region under Grand Commandant Zhu Long and mastered the Ouyang school of the Documents.

sentences.zh: 初，牟氏章句浮辭繁多，[41]有四十五萬餘言，奐減為九萬言。

translations.literal: At first the Mou clan’s chapter-and-verse commentary had floating phrases numerous and excessive, [41] had four hundred fifty thousand-plus words; Huan reduced it to ninety thousand words.
translations.idiomatic: The Mou commentary on the Documents had swollen past four hundred fifty thousand characters of empty gloss; [41] Zhang Huan pared it to ninety thousand.

sentences.zh: 後辟大將軍梁冀府，乃上書桓帝，奏其章句，詔下東觀。

translations.literal: Later summoned to Grand General Liang Ji’s bureau, then submitted memorial to Emperor Huan, presented his chapter-and-verse; edict sent it down to Eastern Vista.
translations.idiomatic: Summoned to Liang Ji’s headquarters, he presented his digest to Emperor Huan; an edict ordered it archived in the Eastern Vista library.

sentences.zh: 以疾去官，復舉賢良，對策第一，擢拜議郎。

translations.literal: Because of illness left office; again recommended worthy and good, examination reply first, promoted gentleman consultant.
translations.idiomatic: Illness forced him home, but he was nominated again as worthy-and-upright, topped the examination, and was appointed gentleman consultant.

sentences.zh: 永壽元年，遷安定屬國都尉。

translations.literal: First year of Yongshou, transferred Dependent State Commandant of Anding.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongshou 1 (155 CE) he became commandant of the dependent state attached to Anding.

sentences.zh: 初到職，而南匈奴左薁鞬台耆、且渠伯德等七千餘人寇美稷，東羌復舉種應之，而奐壁唯有二百許人，聞即勒兵而出。

translations.literal: Just arrived at duty, then Southern Xiongnu Left Awugi Taiqi, Qiju Bode and others more than seven thousand raided Meiji; Eastern Qiang again raised tribes responding to them; yet Huan’s rampart had only about two hundred men—he heard and immediately marshaled troops and went out.
translations.idiomatic: On his first day in office over seven thousand Southern Xiongnu under Taiqi of the Left Awugi clan and Qiju Bode struck Meiji, while eastern Qiang tribes rose in support. Huan had barely two hundred men in camp but marched out the moment word arrived.

sentences.zh: 軍吏以為力不敵，叩頭爭止之。

translations.literal: Army clerks thought strength not a match, knocked heads competing to stop him.
translations.idiomatic: His officers argued they were outmatched and threw themselves at his feet to block the sortie.

sentences.zh: 奐不聽，遂進屯長城，收集兵士，遣將王□招誘東羌，因據龜茲，[42]使南匈奴不得交通東羌。

translations.literal: Huan did not listen; thereupon advanced and camped at the Long Wall, gathered soldiers, dispatched general Wang (text damaged:) to summon and entice Eastern Qiang, thereby occupied Qiuci, [42] causing Southern Xiongnu unable to communicate with Eastern Qiang.
translations.idiomatic: Huan refused to listen, pushed forward to camp on the Long Wall, rallied troops, and sent a commander named Wang [given name missing in text] to win over the eastern Qiang; seizing Qiuci county, [42] he severed the Southern Xiongnu line to their eastern allies.

sentences.zh: 諸豪遂相率與奐和親，共擊薁鞬等，連戰破之。

translations.literal: Various chiefs thereupon led one another with Huan making peace and kinship, jointly attacked Awugi and others, continuously battled and defeated them.
translations.idiomatic: Tribal headmen then allied with Huan, joined him against Taiqi’s horde, and broke them in successive fights.

sentences.zh: 伯德惶恐，將其觿降，郡界以寧。

translations.literal: Bode fearful, led his armies and surrendered; the commandery border thereby was peaceful.
translations.idiomatic: Bode panicked, brought his bands in submission, and the commandery knew calm again.

sentences.zh: 注〔1〕龜茲音丘慈，縣名，屬上郡。

translations.literal: Commentary [1]: Qiuci pronounced qiuci, county name, belonging to Shangjun.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Qiuci is read qiuci; it is a county in Shangjun commandery.

sentences.zh: 前書音義曰「龜茲國人來降之，因以名縣」也。

translations.literal: Former Documents Sounds and Meanings says "people of Qiuci state came and surrendered there, therefore named the county".
translations.idiomatic: The Han shu gloss notes that settlers from the kingdom of Qiuci surrendered there, hence the county name.

sentences.zh: 羌豪帥感奐恩德，上馬二十匹，先零酋長又遺金鐻八枚。

translations.literal: Qiang heroic leaders felt Huan’s grace and virtue, presented twenty horses; Xianling chieftain also sent eight metal vessels.
translations.idiomatic: Grateful Qiang leaders offered twenty horses; a Xianling chief added eight golden vessels.

sentences.zh: 奐並受之，〔1〕而召主簿於諸羌前，以酒酹地曰：[43]「使馬如羊，不以入□；

translations.literal: Huan together accepted them, [1] but summoned the chief clerk before the various Qiang, poured wine libating the ground saying: [43] "Make horses like sheep, not thereby enter (text damaged:);"
translations.idiomatic: Huan accepted both gifts [1] yet called his registrar before the tribes, poured a libation, and swore: [43] "Were horses plentiful as sheep they would not enter my stable (text damaged in source);"

sentences.zh: 使金如粟，不以入懷。

translations.literal: Make gold like millet, not thereby enter the bosom."
translations.idiomatic: were gold common as grain it would not fill my purse."

sentences.zh: 」悉以金馬還之。

translations.literal: " All with gold and horses returned them.
translations.idiomatic: " He returned every horse and ingot.

sentences.zh: [44]羌性貪而貴吏清，前有八都尉率好財貨，為所患苦，及奐正身絜己，威化大行。

translations.literal: [44] Qiang nature is greedy yet esteems officials pure; previously eight commandants generally loved wealth and goods, were harassed by them; when Huan rectified body and cleansed self, awesome transformation greatly proceeded.
translations.idiomatic: [44] The Qiang are grasping yet respect an honest yamen; eight successive commandants had enriched themselves and earned their hatred. Huan kept his own hands clean, and his moral authority transformed the frontier.

sentences.zh: 遷使匈奴中郎將。

translations.literal: Transferred general of the household to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted general of the household for dealings with the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 時休屠各[45]及朔方烏桓並同反叛，燒度遼將軍門，[46]引屯赤坑，鞭火相望。

translations.literal: At that time Xiuchuge [45] and Shuofang Wuhuan together rebelled, burned General Who Crosses the Liao’s gate, [46] drew camp at Red Pit, whip-fires looked at each other.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiuchuge tribesmen [45] and the Wuhuan of Shuofang rose together, torched the headquarters of the General Who Crosses the Liao, [46] and ringed Red Pit with beacon fires.

sentences.zh: 兵觿大恐，各欲亡去。

translations.literal: Troops and armies greatly feared, each wished to flee away.
translations.idiomatic: The army panicked; men wanted to bolt.

sentences.zh: 奐安坐帷中，與弟子講誦自若，軍士稍安。

translations.literal: Huan calmly sat in the curtain, with disciples lecturing and reciting as usual; soldiers gradually calmed.
translations.idiomatic: Huan stayed seated in his tent lecturing pupils as if nothing were wrong, and the ranks slowly steadied.

sentences.zh: 乃潛誘烏桓陰與和通，遂使斬屠各渠帥，襲破其觿。

translations.literal: Thereupon he secretly won the Wuhuan to a covert pact, then had them execute the Xiuchuge chieftains and routed their army by surprise.
translations.idiomatic: He secretly treated with the Wuhuan, then had them execute the Xiuchuge chiefs and surprise their host.

sentences.zh: 諸胡悉降。

translations.literal: Various Hu all surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: Every Hu band submitted.

sentences.zh: 延熹元年，鮮卑寇邊，奐率南單于擊之，斬首數百級。

translations.literal: First year of Yanxi, Xianbei raided the border; Huan led the Southern Shanyu attacked them, beheaded several hundred ranks.
translations.idiomatic: In Yanxi 1 (158 CE) the Xianbei struck the frontier; Huan marched with the Southern Shanyu and took several hundred heads.

sentences.zh: 明年，梁冀被誅，奐以故吏免官禁錮。

translations.literal: Next year Liang Ji was executed; Huan as former clerk was dismissed from office and confined.
translations.idiomatic: The next year Liang Ji died under sentence of death; as his former subordinate Huan was stripped of rank and barred from office.

sentences.zh: 奐與皇甫規友善，奐既被錮，凡諸交舊莫敢為言，唯規薦舉前後七上。

translations.literal: Huan and Huangfu Gui were friendly and good; once Huan was confined, all associates old dared not speak for him; only Gui recommended him front and back seven submissions.
translations.idiomatic: Huan and Huangfu Gui were close; when the proscription hit, former colleagues fell silent—only Gui memorialized seven times on his behalf.

sentences.zh: 在家四歲，復拜武威太守。

translations.literal: At home four years; again appointed Wuwei governor.
translations.idiomatic: After four years at home he was named governor of Wuwei.

sentences.zh: 平均徭賦，率厲散敗，常為諸郡最，河西由是而全。

translations.literal: Evened corvée and taxes, led and urged scattered defeat, constantly was first among various commanderies; Hexi thereby was preserved.
translations.idiomatic: He balanced labor levies and taxes, rallied broken communities, and topped every commandery’s record for good rule—Hexi survived the turmoil because of him.

sentences.zh: 其俗多妖忌，凡二月、五月產子及與父母同月生者，悉殺之。

translations.literal: Their customs abounded in uncanny taboos: infants born in the second or fifth lunar month, or in the same month as a parent, were all killed.
translations.idiomatic: Local superstition demanded that infants born in the second or fifth month—or in the same month as a parent—be put to death.

sentences.zh: 奐示以義方，嚴加賞罰，風俗遂改，百姓生為立祠。

translations.literal: Huan showed them righteous norms, strictly applied rewards and punishments; customs then changed, common people while alive made shrines for him.
translations.idiomatic: He taught humane principle, enforced clear rewards and penalties, and the horror ceased—the people raised shrines to him in his lifetime.

sentences.zh: 舉尤異，遷度遼將軍。

translations.literal: Recommended outstanding and unusual; transferred General Who Crosses the Liao.
translations.idiomatic: Cited for exceptional merit, he became General Who Crosses the Liao.

sentences.zh: 數載閒，幽、并清靜。

translations.literal: Within several years You and Bing were clear and quiet.
translations.idiomatic: Within a few years Youzhou and Bingzhou lay quiet.

sentences.zh: 九年春，徵拜大司農。

translations.literal: Spring of the ninth year, summoned and appointed grand minister of agriculture.
translations.idiomatic: In the spring of the ninth year of Yanxi (166 CE) he was recalled as grand minister of agriculture.

sentences.zh: 鮮卑聞奐去，其夏，遂招結南匈奴、烏桓數道入塞，或五六千騎，或三四千騎，寇掠緣邊九郡，殺略百姓。

translations.literal: Xianbei heard Huan left; that summer thereupon summoned and joined Southern Xiongnu, Wuhuan several routes entering passes, either five or six thousand horse or three or four thousand horse, raiding and plundering nine border commanderies along the edge, killing and seizing common people.
translations.idiomatic: Learning that Huan had left the frontier, the Xianbei that summer enlisted the Southern Xiongnu and Wuhuan for multi-pronged raids—columns of five or six thousand horsemen or three or four thousand—striking nine border commanderies and slaughtering the populace.

sentences.zh: 秋，鮮卑復率八九千騎入塞，誘引東羌與共盟詛。

translations.literal: Autumn, Xianbei again led eight or nine thousand cavalry entered passes, enticed and led Eastern Qiang to jointly swear alliance curse.
translations.idiomatic: That autumn they poured eight or nine thousand riders through the passes and drew the eastern Qiang into blood oaths against the Han.

sentences.zh: 於是上郡沈氐、安定先零諸種共寇武威、張掖，緣邊大被其毒。

translations.literal: Thereupon Shangjun Chenshi, Anding Xianling various kinds jointly raided Wuwei, Zhangye; along the border greatly suffered their poison.
translations.idiomatic: Chenshi bands from Shangjun and Xianling clans from Anding then struck Wuwei and Zhangye, spreading devastation along the whole frontier belt.

sentences.zh: 朝廷以為憂，復拜奐為護匈奴中郎將，以九卿秩督幽、并、涼三州及度遼、烏桓二營，[47]兼察刺史、二千石能否，賞賜甚厚。

translations.literal: The court considered it worry, again appointed Huan general of the household protecting the Xiongnu, with nine ministers rank supervising You, Bing, Liang three prefectures and General Who Crosses the Liao, Wuhuan two camps, [47] concurrently inspecting governors and two-thousand-shi capable or not; rewards and gifts very generous.
translations.idiomatic: Alarmed, the court sent Huan back as general of the household over the Xiongnu at nine-minister rank to oversee You, Bing, and Liang plus the two frontier corps at Liaodong and among the Wuhuan; [47] he was also to judge the performance of inspectors and grandees, and received lavish gifts.

sentences.zh: 匈奴、烏桓聞奐至，因相率還降，凡二十萬口。

translations.literal: Xiongnu, Wuhuan heard Huan arrived, therefore led one another returning to surrender, in all two hundred thousand mouths.
translations.idiomatic: When Huan arrived the Xiongnu and Wuhuan submitted in a body—some two hundred thousand people.

sentences.zh: 奐但誅其首惡，餘皆慰納之。

translations.literal: Huan only executed their chief evils; the rest all consoled and accepted them.
translations.idiomatic: He executed only the ringleaders and pacified the rest.

sentences.zh: 唯鮮卑出塞去。

translations.literal: Only Xianbei exited passes and left.
translations.idiomatic: Only the Xianbei withdrew beyond the frontier.

sentences.zh: 永康元年春，東羌、先零五六千騎寇關中，圍祋祤，掠雲陽。

translations.literal: Spring first year of Yongkang, Eastern Qiang, Xianling five or six thousand cavalry raided Guanzhong, besieged Duyi, plundered Yunyang.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongkang 1 (167 CE) eastern Qiang and Xianling horsemen—five or six thousand strong—thundered into Guanzhong, surrounded Duyi, and looted Yunyang.

sentences.zh: 夏，復攻沒兩營，殺千餘人。

translations.literal: Summer, again attacked and overwhelmed two camps, killed more than a thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: That summer they overran two garrisons and killed over a thousand troops.

sentences.zh: 冬，羌岸尾、摩蟞等[48]脅同種復鈔三輔。

translations.literal: Winter, Qiang Anwei, Mobie and others [48] coerced same kind again raided Three Adjuncts.
translations.idiomatic: In winter Anwei, Mobie, and other Qiang chiefs [48] forced their kinsmen into fresh raids on the Three Adjuncts.

sentences.zh: 奐遣司馬尹端、董卓並擊，大破之，斬其酋豪，首虜萬餘人，三州清定。

translations.literal: Huan dispatched Majors Yin Duan, Dong Zhuo jointly attacked, greatly defeated them, beheaded their chieftain heroes, heads and captives more than ten thousand persons; three prefectures clear and settled.
translations.idiomatic: Huan sent Majors Yin Duan and Dong Zhuo against them; they shattered the alliance, executed its leaders, and counted over ten thousand heads and captives—You, Bing, and Liang were calm again.

sentences.zh: 論功當封，奐不事宦官，故賞遂不行，唯賜錢二十萬，除家一人為郎。

translations.literal: Discussing merit ought to enfeoff; Huan did not serve eunuchs, therefore rewards then did not proceed; only gifted cash two hundred thousand, appointed one family member as gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Merit called for a fief, but Huan would not court the eunuchs, so honors stopped at two hundred thousand cash and a gentleman post for one kinsman.

sentences.zh: 並辭不受，而願徙屬弘農華陰。

translations.literal: Both declined and did not accept, but wished to transfer registering to Hongnong Huayin.
translations.idiomatic: He refused both offers and asked instead to register his household in Huayin in Hongnong.

sentences.zh: 舊制邊人不得內移，唯奐因功特聽，故始為弘農人焉。

translations.literal: Old system: border people could not inwardly shift; only Huan because of merit specially permitted; therefore began to be Hongnong person.
translations.idiomatic: Frontier households were forbidden to move inward, but Huan’s service earned a unique waiver—so his line became Huayin natives.

sentences.zh: 明年夏，青蛇見於御坐軒前，[49]又大風雨雹，霹靂拔樹，詔使百僚各言災應。

translations.literal: Next summer, green snake appeared before imperial seat pavilion front, [49] again great wind rain hail, thunderbolt uprooted trees; edict ordered hundred officials each speak disaster responses.
translations.idiomatic: The following summer a green snake appeared before the imperial carriage porch [49] and violent storms—wind, hail, and lightning that tore up trees—prompted an edict bidding every minister interpret the omens.

sentences.zh: 奐上疏曰：「臣聞風為號令，動物通氣。

translations.literal: Huan submitted memorial saying: "Your servant has heard wind is command and order, moving things penetrate qi."
translations.idiomatic: Huan memorialized: "I am told the wind carries the ruler’s commands and stirs the realm’s vital breath."

sentences.zh: [50]木生於火，相須乃明。

translations.literal: [50] Wood is born from fire; the two depend on each other for brightness.
translations.idiomatic: [50] Wood arises from fire—the two must sustain each other before the flame shines clear.

sentences.zh: 蛇能屈申，配龍騰蟄。

translations.literal: The snake can coil and stretch; it pairs with the dragon that soars or lies in hibernation.
translations.idiomatic: The snake bends and straightens like the dragon that mounts to heaven or sleeps in the deeps.

sentences.zh: [51]順至為休征，逆來為殃咎。

translations.literal: [51] When compliance arrives it is an auspicious sign; when opposition comes it is calamity and fault.
translations.idiomatic: [51] Obedience to heaven brings blessed omens; defiance brings disaster.

sentences.zh: 陰氣專用，則凝精為雹。

translations.literal: When yin qi monopolizes influence, its essence congeals into hail.
translations.idiomatic: When yin force rules unchecked, its chill falls as hail.

sentences.zh: 故大將軍竇武、太傅陳蕃，或志寧社稷，或方直不回，前以讒勝，並伏誅戮，海內默默，人懷震憤。

translations.literal: Therefore Grand General Dou Wu and Grand Tutor Chen Fan—one whose aim was to secure the altars of state, one who was square and upright and would not bend—were lately overcome by slander and put to death together; within the seas all fell silent, yet every heart harbored shock and anger.
translations.idiomatic: Grand General Dou Wu and Grand Tutor Chen Fan—one steadied the dynasty, the other stood incorrupt—were destroyed because calumny prevailed. The empire went mute, but rage smoldered in every breast.

sentences.zh: 昔周公葬不如禮，天乃動威。

translations.literal: Formerly, when the Duke of Zhou’s burial did not accord with ritual, heaven displayed its awesome power.
translations.idiomatic: When the Duke of Zhou was buried short of proper rite, heaven answered with terrifying signs.

sentences.zh: [52]今武、蕃忠貞，未被明宥，妖眚之來，皆為此也。

translations.literal: [52] Now Wu and Fan were loyal and steadfast, yet have not received clear pardon; the coming of uncanny calamities is all because of this.
translations.idiomatic: [52] Dou and Chen died faithful men still denied honorable amnesty—these uncanny visitations spring from that wrong.

sentences.zh: 宜急為改葬，徙還家屬。

translations.literal: Ought urgently for them change burial, move back family dependents.
translations.idiomatic: Rebury them at once and restore their kin to their homes."

sentences.zh: 其從坐禁錮，一切蠲除。

translations.literal: Those implicated and confined by association—all such bans were lifted.
translations.idiomatic: Everyone punished by guilt-by-association was pardoned outright.

sentences.zh: 又皇太后雖居南居，而恩禮不接，朝臣莫言，遠近失望。

translations.literal: Moreover although the empress dowager dwells in the southern palace, favor and ritual do not reach her; court officials none speak; near and far are disappointed.
translations.idiomatic: Though the empress dowager lodges in the southern palace, she receives neither kindness nor courtesy; officials dare not speak for her, and disappointment runs from capital to countryside.

sentences.zh: 宜思大義顧復之報。

translations.literal: One ought to ponder great righteousness and repay the care she showed in raising you.
translations.idiomatic: Remember the debt a son owes the mother who bore and nursed him.

sentences.zh: 」[53]天子深納奐言，以問諸黃門常侍，左右皆惡之，帝不得自從。

translations.literal: The memorial closed. [53] The Son of Heaven deeply accepted Huan’s words and consulted the Yellow Gate attendants; those at his side loathed the proposal, so the emperor could not follow his own mind.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Huan fell silent, the emperor embraced his advice—until he aired it before the eunuchs, who recoiled; in the end he could not act as his conscience urged.

sentences.zh: 轉奐太常，與尚書劉猛、刁韙、□良同薦王暢、李膺可參三公之選，而曹節等彌疾其言，遂下詔切責之。

translations.literal: Huan was transferred to grand master of ceremonies; together with Masters of Writing Liu Meng, Diao Wei, and Liang he recommended Wang Chang and Li Ying as candidates for the Three Excellencies—but Cao Jie and his circle hated the suggestion all the more, and an edict sharply rebuked them.
translations.idiomatic: Promoted grand sacrificer, Huan joined Masters of Writing Liu Meng, Diao Wei, and Liang in urging Wang Chang and Li Ying for the highest offices; Cao Jie’s faction exploded with rage and forced down an edict of stern censure.

sentences.zh: 奐等皆自囚廷尉，數日乃得出，並以三月俸贖罪。

translations.literal: Huan and the others voluntarily surrendered to the Commandant of Justice; only after several days could they leave, each redeeming the offense with three months’ salary.
translations.idiomatic: They booked themselves into the Commandant’s jail; days later they bought release by forfeiting three months’ pay.

sentences.zh: 司隸校尉王寓，出於宦官，欲借寵公卿，以求薦舉，百僚畏憚，莫不許諾，唯奐獨拒之。

translations.literal: Colonel Director of Retainers Wang Yu, a creature of the eunuchs, sought borrowed prestige from the great ministers to win recommendation; the bureaucracy feared him and all promised—only Huan refused.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Yu, the colonel’s eunuch-backed appointmentee, tried to trade on ministerial favor for promotion; officials cringed into consent—Huan alone declined.

sentences.zh: 寓怒，因此遂陷以黨罪，禁錮歸田里。

translations.literal: Yu was furious and therefore framed him with a faction crime, confined him and sent him home to the fields.
translations.idiomatic: Enraged, Yu pinned a partisan label on him and barred him from office for life.

sentences.zh: 奐前為度遼將軍，與段熲爭擊羌，不相平。

translations.literal: Huan had formerly been General Who Crosses the Liao and quarreled with Duan Jiong over campaigning against the Qiang—they were not at peace.
translations.idiomatic: As former commander on the Liao frontier Huan had clashed with Duan Jiong over strategy against the Qiang; bad blood lingered.

sentences.zh: 及熲為司隸校尉，欲逐奐歸敦煌，將害之。

translations.literal: When Jiong became colonel director of retainers he wished to drive Huan back to Dunhuang and meant to destroy him.
translations.idiomatic: Once Jiong took the colonelcy he plotted to banish Huan to Dunhuang and finish him there.

sentences.zh: 奐憂懼，奏記謝熲曰：「小人不明，得過州將，千里委命，以情相歸。

translations.literal: Huan, fearful, submitted a letter apologizing to Jiong: "This petty man was unclear; I offended the provincial commander; from a thousand li I entrust my life, returning feeling to you."
translations.idiomatic: Terrified, Huan sent Jiong a humble note: "I was a fool to cross your predecessor as inspector; I throw myself on your mercy from a thousand li away."

sentences.zh: [54]足下仁篤，照其辛苦，使人未反，復獲郵書。

translations.literal: [54] Your benevolence is deep; you took note of my hardship—before my envoy had returned I received another letter by post.
translations.idiomatic: [54] Your kindness saw my distress—before my messenger even returned a second letter reached me."

sentences.zh: 恩詔分明，前以寫白，而州期切促，郡縣惶懼，屏營延企，側待歸命。

translations.literal: The gracious edict was clear; I formerly wrote and explained, yet the province’s deadline presses urgently; commanderies and counties tremble in fear; I wait trembling, standing on tiptoe, tilting to await returning to obedience.
translations.idiomatic: The benevolent order was plain—I reported as ordered—but the provincial office hounds us on deadline; yamen clerks panic; I hover on edge awaiting your word."

sentences.zh: 父母朽骨，孤魂相托，若蒙鄉憐，壹惠咳唾，則澤流黃泉，施及冥寞，非奐生死所能報塞。

translations.literal: Parents’ decayed bones, orphaned souls entrusted—if I receive your pity, one favor of spit, then moisture flows to Yellow Springs, bounty reaches the dark silent; not what Huan alive or dead can repay.
translations.idiomatic: My parents’ bones lie bare and their spirits look to you for shelter; a cough’s worth of mercy from you would reach them under the yellow springs—more than I could repay in life or death."

sentences.zh: 夫無毛髮之勞，而欲求人丘山之用，此淳于髡所以拍髀仰天而笑者也。

translations.literal: Now without hair-and-fur’s labor yet wishing to seek from others hill-and-mound’s service—this is why Chunyu Kun slapped thigh looking up to heaven and laughed.
translations.idiomatic: To ask mountain-high favors after feather-light effort is why Chunyu Kun slapped his thigh and roared with laughter at heaven.

sentences.zh: [55]誠知言必見譏，然猶未能無望。

translations.literal: [55] Truly I know speech will certainly meet ridicule, yet still cannot be without hope.
translations.idiomatic: [55] I know plain speech invites mockery—yet hope dies hard."

sentences.zh: 何者？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 朽骨無益於人，而文王葬之；

translations.literal: Decayed bones without benefit to others—yet King Wen buried them;
translations.idiomatic: King Wen buried bones that could no longer help anyone;

sentences.zh: [56]死馬無所復用，而燕昭寶之。

translations.literal: [56] dead horse had nothing further for use, yet King Zhao of Yan treasured it.
translations.idiomatic: [56] King Zhao of Yan prized a dead horse no workshop could use.

sentences.zh: [57]黨同文、昭之德，豈不大哉！

translations.literal: [57] Align with Wen’s and Zhao’s virtue—is it not great!
translations.idiomatic: [57] To match the generosity of Wen and Zhao—could any deed be greater?

sentences.zh: [58]凡人之情，冤則呼天，窮則叩心。

translations.literal: [58] As for ordinary people’s feeling: wronged then call heaven; exhausted then knock heart.
translations.idiomatic: [58] Mortals cry to heaven when wronged and beat their breasts when driven to the wall.

sentences.zh: 今呼天不聞，叩心無益，誠自傷痛。

translations.literal: Now calling heaven not heard, knocking heart without benefit—truly I wound myself in pain.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven does not hear me; beating my breast avails nothing—I ache with grief.

sentences.zh: 俱生聖世，獨為匪人。

translations.literal: Together born in sagely age, alone made wicked person.
translations.idiomatic: We live under a sage Son of Heaven, yet I alone am cast out as vile.

sentences.zh: [59]孤微之人，無所告訴。

translations.literal: [59] Orphaned petty person, nowhere to complain.
translations.idiomatic: [59] A nobody like me has nowhere to plead.

sentences.zh: 如不哀憐，便為魚肉。

translations.literal: If not pitied, then become fish and meat.
translations.idiomatic: Without your pity I am knife and board.

sentences.zh: [60]企心東望，無所復言。

translations.literal: [60] Standing heart east gazing, nothing further to say.
translations.idiomatic: [60] I lift my eyes east toward Luoyang and have said all I can."

sentences.zh: 」熲雖剛猛，省書哀之，卒不忍也。

translations.literal: " Jiong although fierce and harsh, reading the letter pitied him, in the end could not bear it.
translations.idiomatic: For all his harshness, Jiong read the plea with pity and could not bring himself to destroy him.

sentences.zh: 時禁錮者多不能守靜，或死或徙。

translations.literal: At that time many who were confined could not keep quiet—some died, some were banished.
translations.idiomatic: Most men under proscription could not sit still—some fled, some died in the attempt.

sentences.zh: 奐閉門不出，養徒千人，著尚書記難三十餘萬言。

translations.literal: Huan closed gate and did not go out, raised disciples a thousand persons, authored Documents Recorded Difficulties more than three hundred thousand words.
translations.idiomatic: Huan stayed behind closed doors, taught a thousand pupils, and wrote well over three hundred thousand characters of commentary on the Documents.

sentences.zh: 奐少立志節，嘗與士友言曰：「大丈夫處世，當為國家立功邊境。

translations.literal: Huan in youth set resolve and integrity; once spoke to scholar friends saying: "Great man in the world ought for the state establish merit on the border."
translations.idiomatic: In youth Huan told friends: "A man of spirit wins honor for the realm on the frontier."

sentences.zh: 」及為將帥，果有勳名。

translations.literal: " When he became general and commander, indeed had merit and fame.
translations.idiomatic: As a commander he earned the renown he had promised.

sentences.zh: 董卓慕之，使其兄遺縑百匹。

translations.literal: Dong Zhuo admired him, had his elder brother present one hundred bolts of silk.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo admired him and sent his elder brother with a gift of a hundred bolts of silk.

sentences.zh: 奐惡卓為人，絕而不受。

translations.literal: Huan loathed Zhuo’s conduct, cut off and did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: Loathing Zhuo’s character, he refused the gift outright.

sentences.zh: 光和四年卒，年七十八。

translations.literal: Died fourth year of Guanghe, age seventy-eight.
translations.idiomatic: He died in Guanghe 4 (181 CE), aged seventy-eight.

sentences.zh: 遺命曰：「吾前後仕進，十要銀艾，[61]不能和光同塵，為讒邪所忌。

translations.literal: Death testament said: "I front and back entered office, ten times wore silver and mugwort seals, [61] could not harmonize light with dust, was envied by slanderous evil."
translations.idiomatic: His final instructions read: "Ten times I wore ministerial insignia [61] yet never learned to blend with the crowd—so slander brought me down."

sentences.zh: [62]通塞命也，始終常也。

translations.literal: [62] Open blocked is fate; beginning end is constant.
translations.idiomatic: [62] Success and setback are heaven’s lot; birth and death are the constant way.

sentences.zh: 但地厎冥冥，長無曉期，而復纏以纊撓，牢以釘密，為不喜耳。
