translations.idiomatic: The purge tally—Liang Ji, Deng kin, and the partisan roster.

sentences.zh: 無德而壟曰嬖，謂廢鄧皇后也。

translations.literal: To monopolize favor without virtue is bi; it refers to deposing Empress Deng.
translations.idiomatic: Bi favorite without merit—the deposition of Empress Deng.

sentences.zh: 殺桂陽太守任胤，殺南陽太守成焰、太原太守劉質等也。

translations.literal: Killing Guiyang Administrator Ren Yin; Nanyang Administrator Cheng Yan; Taiyuan Administrator Liu Zhi; and so on.
translations.idiomatic: Administrator murders—Ren, Cheng, Liu, and peers.

sentences.zh: 漢官儀曰：「矩字叔方。」

translations.idiomatic: The Han guan yi records Ju styled Shufang.

sentences.zh: 古本反。

translations.idiomatic: Phonetic note: gu plus ben.

translations.idiomatic: Gou: to hook forth precedent.

sentences.zh: 謂李膺等事也。

translations.literal: It refers to Li Ying and the rest.
translations.idiomatic: The Li Ying affair and cohort.

sentences.zh: 怍，籩也。

translations.literal: Zuo means shame; embarrassment; commentarial lemma.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo: mortification—the standard gloss reads shame.

translations.idiomatic: Jinchang County northeast of Guazhou.

sentences.zh: 時牟卿受書於張堪，為博士，故有牟氏章句。

translations.idiomatic: Mou Qing’s exegetical line—trained under Zhang Kan as boshi.

sentences.zh: 龜茲音丘慈，縣名，屬上郡。

translations.idiomatic: Qiuci County—Shang commandery outpost.

sentences.zh: 前書音義曰「龜茲國人來降之，因以名縣」也。

translations.literal: The Former Han pronunciation gloss says: "Qiuci people came to surrender, hence the county was named."
translations.idiomatic: The toponym recalls surrendered Qiuci settlers.

sentences.zh: 以酒沃地謂之酹。

translations.literal: Pouring wine upon the ground is called lei.
translations.idiomatic: Libation pour—lei on the earth.

sentences.zh: 音力外反。

translations.literal: Fanqie reading: li-wai fan.
translations.idiomatic: Phonetic: li plus wai.

sentences.zh: 如羊如粟，喻多也。

translations.literal: Like sheep, like millet—a metaphor for multitude.
translations.idiomatic: Stock metaphor—flocks and grain sheaves of plenty.

sentences.zh: 屠音直於反。

translations.idiomatic: Wuyuan billet for the Liao-crossing general.

translations.literal: In Yongping 8 of Mingdi the General Who Crosses the Liao was first established, stationed at Man lacuna County in Wuyuan commandery; the Han Official Regulations says "the Wuhuan Colonel was stationed at Ning County in Shanggu," hence they are called the two camps.
translations.idiomatic: Twin frontier posts: the Liao general at Wuyuan and the Wuhuan colonel at Shanggu.

sentences.zh: 蟞音必薛反。

translations.idiomatic: Hall thresholds—the carved sill rails called xuan.

translations.literal: The Yi clan wind-horn treatise says: "Wind is Heaven’s command, whereby it reprimands and warns the lord."
translations.idiomatic: Yi-style wind divination casts the gale as Heaven’s rebuke.

sentences.zh: 易曰「龍蛇之蟄，以存身也。

translations.literal: The Changes says: "Dragon and serpent hibernate in order to preserve the body."
translations.idiomatic: A Zhou Yi line on lying low to endure.

sentences.zh: 」慎子曰「騰蛇游霧，飛龍乘雲，雲罷霧散，與蚯蚓同」也。

translations.literal: Shenzi says: "The soaring serpent roams mist; the soaring dragon rides clouds—when clouds cease and mist scatters, they are the same as earthworms."
translations.idiomatic: Shenzi’s sobering image—without clouds, dragons are worms.

sentences.zh: 尚書大傳：「周公薨，成王欲葬之於成周，天乃雷雨以風，禾即盡偃，大木斯拔，國人大恐。

translations.literal: The Grand Documents Commentary: "When the Duke of Zhou died, King Cheng wished to bury him at Chengzhou; Heaven sent thunder, rain, and wind; the grain lay flat; great trees were uprooted; the capital greatly feared."
translations.idiomatic: Heaven’s tantrum at Chengzhou burial plans for the Duke of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 王葬周公畢，示不敢臣也。」

translations.literal: When the king finished burying the Duke of Zhou, he showed he dared not treat him as a subject.
translations.idiomatic: Ceremony affirmed—the duke honored above mere minister.

sentences.zh: 顧，旋視也。

translations.literal: Gu means to look back; to glance around.
translations.idiomatic: Gu: backward glance.

sentences.zh: 復，反覆也。

translations.literal: Fu means to turn over repeatedly.
translations.idiomatic: Fu: rocking back and forth.

sentences.zh: 小雅曰：「父兮生我，母兮鞠我，顧我復我，出入腹我。」

translations.literal: The Minor Odes says: "Father, you begot me; mother, you nurtured me; you looked back for me, you turned for me; you carried me in and out."
translations.idiomatic: A parental refrain drawn from the Xiao Ya.

sentences.zh: 漢官儀曰：「司隸州部河南雒陽，管三輔、三河、弘農七郡。

translations.literal: The Han Official Regulations says: "The Metropolitan Commander’s province embraced Henan and Luoyang, administering Sanfu, Sanhe, and Hongnong—seven commanderies."
translations.idiomatic: The metropolitan belt spans the capital counties plus seven jurisdictions.

sentences.zh: 」所以奐屈於熲，稱曰「州將」焉。

translations.literal: Hence Huan yielded to Jiong and styled him "provincial commander."
translations.idiomatic: Why Huan bowed to Jiong as zhou jiang.

sentences.zh: 拍音片百反。

translations.literal: Pai fanqie: pian-bai fan.
translations.idiomatic: Reading for pai.

sentences.zh: 髀音步弟反。

translations.idiomatic: Reading note for bi meaning thigh.

translations.literal: The Records of the Grand Historian: Chu raised troops against Qi; King Wei of Qi sent Chunyu Kun bearing one hundred catties of gold and ten quadrigas of horses to Zhao seeking relief.
translations.idiomatic: Shiji vignette—Qi’s Kun dispatched to buy Zhao aid.

sentences.zh: 髡仰天大笑，冠纓索絕。

translations.literal: Kun threw back his head and laughed aloud; his cap strings snapped.
translations.idiomatic: Roaring laughter—cap cords burst.

sentences.zh: 王曰：「先生少之乎？

translations.literal: The king said: Sir, do you find it too little?
translations.idiomatic: The king asks whether he finds the gift too slight?

sentences.zh: 」髡曰：「今者臣從東方來，見道傍有禳田者，操一豚縞，酒一盂，而祝曰：『甌婁滿篝，污邪滿車，五穀蕃熟，穰穰滿家。

translations.literal: Kun said: "Coming from the east I saw beside the road one praying for the fields, holding one suckling pig and one bolt of plain silk, wine in one cup, praying: ‘May upland bins brim and low fields overflow carts; may the five grains flourish and harvest glut our homes.’"
translations.idiomatic: Kun’s roadside parable—meager offering, immense petition.

sentences.zh: 』臣見其所持者狹，所求者奢，故笑。

translations.literal: I laughed because what he held was narrow yet what he sought was extravagant.
translations.idiomatic: The joke was a tiny sacrifice paired with cosmic expectations.

sentences.zh: 」於是王乃益以黃金千鎰、白璧十雙、車馬百駟也。

translations.literal: Thereupon the king increased the gift to a thousand yi of gold, ten pairs of white jade, and one hundred quadrigas.
translations.idiomatic: Qi upped the ante with gold, jade, and a hundred teams.

sentences.zh: 新序曰：「文王作靈臺，掘得死人骨，吏以聞。

translations.literal: The New Prefaces says: "King Wen built the Ling Terrace and unearthed dead men’s bones; officials reported it."
translations.idiomatic: A Xinxu tale of bones under the Ling Terrace.

sentences.zh: 文王曰：『葬之。

translations.literal: King Wen said: ‘Bury them.’
translations.idiomatic: Wen orders decent burial.

sentences.zh: 』吏曰：『此無主矣。

translations.literal: The officials said: ‘These have no master.’
translations.idiomatic: The clerks protest that the bones lack a claimant.

sentences.zh: 』文王曰：『有天下者，天下之主也；

translations.literal: King Wen said: ‘Whoever holds all under Heaven is master of all under Heaven.’
translations.idiomatic: Wen answers that the sovereign tends every soul in the realm.

sentences.zh: 有一國者，一國之主也。

translations.literal: Whoever holds one state is master of that state.
translations.idiomatic: The ruler of a single realm speaks as its sovereign.

sentences.zh: 寡人固其主焉。

translations.literal: I, the unworthy one, am surely master over them.
translations.idiomatic: The lonely throne nonetheless answers for every soul.

sentences.zh: 』令吏以棺葬之。

translations.literal: He ordered the clerks to bury them in coffins.
translations.idiomatic: He commanded proper coffin burial for the bones.

sentences.zh: 天下聞之，曰：『文王賢矣，澤及朽骨，又況人乎。』」

translations.literal: When all under Heaven heard it they said: "King Wen is worthy indeed—his kindness reaches even rotten bones; how much more the living?"
translations.idiomatic: The realm praised Wen for mercy that touched the dead before the quick.

sentences.zh: 黨音佗朗反。

translations.literal: Dang fanqie: tuo-lang fan.
translations.idiomatic: Reading note for dang.

sentences.zh: 詩小雅曰「哀我征夫，獨為匪人」也。

translations.literal: The Minor Odes says: "Pity our campaigning husbands—they alone are ranked less than human."
translations.idiomatic: A Xiao Ya couplet on soldiers treated as expendable.

sentences.zh: 言將為人所吞噬也。

translations.literal: It means they are about to be swallowed up by others.
translations.idiomatic: Figurative prey—about to be consumed.

sentences.zh: 銀印綠綬也，以艾草染之，故曰艾也。

translations.literal: Silver seal with green ribbon; dyed with mugwort, hence called ai.
translations.idiomatic: Green-silk silver seal—the hue earned its mugwort nickname.

sentences.zh: 老子曰「和其光，同其塵」也。

translations.literal: Laozi says: "Blend your radiance; mingle with the dust."
translations.idiomatic: Daodejing counsel to dim brilliance and move among the common.

sentences.zh: 陸翽鄴中記曰：「永嘉末，發齊桓公墓，得水銀池金蠶數十箔，珠襦、玉匣、繒彩不可勝數。

translations.literal: Lu Hui’s Records of Ye says: "At the end of the Yongjia era they opened Duke Huan of Qi’s tomb and found a pool of mercury, dozens of foil packets of golden silkworms, pearl jackets, jade coffin fittings, and silks beyond counting.
translations.idiomatic: Ye chronicle of Yongjia tomb-breaking—mercury pools and stacked grave goods.

sentences.zh: 」左傳曰：「晉文公朝王，請隧。

translations.literal: The Zuo Commentary says: "Duke Wen of Jin attended the king and requested a royal-grade burial tunnel."
translations.idiomatic: Zuo vignette—Jin Wen’s bid for kingly funeral honors.

sentences.zh: 王不許，曰：『王章也，未有代德而有二王，亦叔父之所惡也。

translations.literal: The king refused, saying: "That is royal statute; one who has not replaced virtue yet presumes two kings—this too is what Uncle detests."
translations.idiomatic: The Zhou king rejects a second ritual kingship.

sentences.zh: 』」晉文既臣，請用王禮，是其奢也。

translations.literal: Once Jin Wen had become the king’s subject he still sought royal rites—that was his extravagance.
translations.idiomatic: Even as liege he pressed for imperial ceremony.

sentences.zh: 武帝時，楊王孫死，誡其子為布囊盛屍，入地七尺，脫去其囊，以身親土。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Wu Yang Wangsun died enjoining his son to sew the corpse into cloth sacks, inter seven feet down, strip away the wrapping, and let the body touch earth itself.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Wangsun’s austere burial—cloth sack and naked earth.

sentences.zh: 王愔文志曰：「芝少持高操，以名臣子勤學，文為儒宗，武為將表。

translations.literal: Wang Yin’s Literary Records says: "From youth Zhi held lofty integrity; as the son of a noted minister he studied tirelessly—literary model among scholars, martial exemplar among generals.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Yin’s encomium—Zhi as paragon of brush and blade.

sentences.zh: 太尉辟，公車有道徵，皆不至，號張有道。

translations.literal: The Grand Commandant summoned him; the imperial coaches summoned him as a man of integrity—he answered neither, and was styled Zhang the Man of the Way.
translations.idiomatic: Shunned high summons—hence "Zhang Youdao."

sentences.zh: 尤好草書，學崔、杜之法，家之衣帛，必書而後練。

translations.literal: He especially loved cursive script, studying Cui and Du’s methods; bolts of silk at home had to be written on before dyeing.
translations.idiomatic: Calligraphy zeal—scribbled every bolt before it saw the vat.

sentences.zh: 臨池學書，水為之黑。

translations.literal: Practicing by the pond darkened the water with ink.
translations.idiomatic: Ink-blackened pool from endless practice.

sentences.zh: 下筆則為楷則，號□□不暇草書，為世所寶，寸紙不遺，韋仲將謂之『草聖』也。」

translations.literal: Whatever his brush touched became the norm for regular script; styled lacuna in text—he had no leisure for wild cursive—treasured by the age so no scrap was discarded; Wei Zhongjiang titled him "sage of cursive."
translations.idiomatic: His regular hand set the standard; contemporaries crowned him cursive sage.

sentences.zh: 宦者鄭觿封鄛鄉侯也。

translations.literal: The eunuch Zheng Yi was enfeoffed as marquis of Chao Township.
translations.idiomatic: Palace favorite Zheng Yi’s Chao-xiang fief.

sentences.zh: 奐被曹節等矯制，使率五營士圍殺陳蕃、竇武等。

translations.literal: Huan fell victim when Cao Jie and others forged edicts and made him lead five-camp troops to besiege and kill Chen Fan, Dou Wu, and company.
translations.idiomatic: Coerced purge—Huan’s troops trapped Chen Fan and Dou Wu.

sentences.zh: 詩國風也。

translations.literal: From the Airs of the States in the Odes.
translations.idiomatic: A Guofeng citation.

sentences.zh: 啜，泣貌也，音知劣反。

translations.idiomatic: Chuo: tear-stained face; phonetic gloss attached.

sentences.zh: *〔會〕*宗字子松，天水上邽人，元帝時為西域都護。

translations.literal: Marginal reading Hui: Zong, courtesy name Zisong, a man of Shanggui in Tianshui, served as Western Regions Protector-General under Emperor Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: Duan Huizong—Yuan-era protector of the Western Regions.

sentences.zh: 死，城郭諸國為發喪立祠。

translations.literal: When he died the walled states held mourning for him and erected shrines.
translations.idiomatic: Frontier polities mourned him like a patron.

translations.idiomatic: Shun’s burial mound at Xianling.

sentences.zh: 陽陵，景帝陵。

translations.literal: Yangling—Emperor Jing’s tomb.
translations.idiomatic: Jing’s Yangling sepulcher.

translations.idiomatic: Old Pengyang maps to Pengyuan.

translations.literal: It refers to lacuna—the twelve earthly branches.

translations.idiomatic: Lingwu Gorge northwest of Huaiyuan.

sentences.zh: 淮南子曰「申包胥曾繭重胝」也。

translations.literal: The Huainanzi says: "Shen Baoxu cocooned his feet and layered his calluses."
translations.idiomatic: Huainanzi image of relentless marching.

sentences.zh: 縣名，屬安定郡。

translations.literal: A county name subordinate to Anding commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Another Anding county seat.

sentences.zh: 傷敗曰磨，音女六反。

translations.literal: Mo names routed defeat; fanqie nü-liu fan.
translations.idiomatic: Mo: shattered ranks; phonetic gloss.

sentences.zh: 言羌亦稟天之一氣所生，誅之不可盡也。

translations.literal: It says the Qiang likewise spring from Heaven’s single qi—you cannot slaughter them to extinction.
translations.idiomatic: They share humanity’s breath—annihilation is fantasy.

sentences.zh: 假，借也。

translations.literal: Jia means to lend; to borrow.
translations.idiomatic: Jia: Heaven “lends” another’s hand.

sentences.zh: 尚書曰「皇天降災，假手於我有命」也。

translations.literal: The Documents say: "High Heaven sent calamity, lending the agency to our appointed house."
translations.idiomatic: Shangshu line on delegated punishment.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰「衛大旱，卜有事於山川，不吉。

translations.literal: The Zuo Commentary says: "Wei suffered great drought; divination for sacrifice to hills and rivers proved ill."
translations.idiomatic: Zuo drought episode opens.

sentences.zh: 寧莊子曰：『昔周饑，克殷而年豐。

translations.literal: Ning Zhuangzi said: "Long ago Zhou starved yet conquered Yin and afterward enjoyed bumper harvests."
translations.idiomatic: Precedent—conquest after famine.

sentences.zh: 今邢方無道，天欲衛伐邢乎？

translations.literal: Now Xing flouts the Way—does Heaven mean Wei to chastise Xing?
translations.idiomatic: Heaven’s drought as mandate against Xing?

sentences.zh: 』從之，師興而雨」也。

translations.literal: They followed his counsel; the host mobilized and rain fell.
translations.idiomatic: Campaign launched; skies opened.

sentences.zh: 占，候也。

translations.literal: Zhan means to watch; to take omens.
translations.idiomatic: Zhan: scan the signs.

sentences.zh: 克，勝也。

translations.literal: Ke means to conquer; to prevail.
translations.idiomatic: Ke: decisive victory.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰「師克在和不在觿」也。

translations.literal: The Zuo Commentary says: "An army prevails through harmony, not through sheer numbers."
translations.idiomatic: Classic maxim—unity beats mass (graph variant in source).

sentences.zh: 獷，惡魍也，音谷猛反。

translations.literal: Guang means fierce, ugly aspect; fanqie gu-meng fan.
translations.idiomatic: Savage mien—possibly 貌 miswritten as 魍 in source.

sentences.zh: 宣帝時，充國擊西羌，徙之於金城郡也。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Xuan Chongguo attacked the western Qiang and moved them into Jincheng Commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan-era resettlement into Jincheng.

sentences.zh: 遷置天水、隴西、扶風，見西羌傳也。

translations.literal: Later removals to Tianshui, Longxi, and Fufeng appear in the Western Qiang biography.
translations.idiomatic: See Western Qiang chapter for later shifts.

sentences.zh: 「鯁」與「梗」同。

translations.literal: "Geng" is the same graph family as "geng" meaning obstruction.
translations.idiomatic: Orthographic note—geng equals stubborn blockage.

sentences.zh: 梗，病也。

translations.literal: Geng means ailment; affliction.
translations.idiomatic: Geng: chronic harm.

sentences.zh: 大雅云：「至今為梗。」

translations.literal: The Greater Odes says: "Even now it remains our affliction."
translations.idiomatic: Da Ya verse on enduring harm.

sentences.zh: 殖，生也。

translations.literal: Zhi means to grow; to propagate.
translations.idiomatic: Zhi: spring back like weeds.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「為國家者，見惡如農夫之務去草焉，絕其本根，勿使能殖。」

translations.literal: The Zuo Commentary says: "Those who steward a state, seeing evil, should be like farmers intent on rooting weeds—sever the taproot so none may sprout again."
translations.idiomatic: Zuo agrarian metaphor for extirpating rebels.

sentences.zh: 杜預注左傳曰：「燼，火余木也。」

translations.literal: Du Yu annotating the Zuo Commentary says: "Jin is charcoal left after fire."
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu gloss—jin as embers.

sentences.zh: 御，制御也。

translations.literal: Yu means to restrain; to command.
translations.idiomatic: Yu: steer from afar.

sentences.zh: 淮南子曰「國不可從外理，軍不可從中御」也。

translations.literal: The Huainanzi says: "A state cannot be administered by outsiders; an army cannot be commanded from deep within the court."
translations.idiomatic: Huainanzi warning against remote meddling.

sentences.zh: 西縣屬天水郡，故城在今秦州上邽縣西南也。

translations.literal: Xi County belonged to Tianshui commandery; its old walls lie southwest of Shanggui County in Qinzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Xi County ruins southwest of Shanggui.

sentences.zh: 郭璞曰：「蓐，席也。

translations.literal: Guo Pu says: "Ru is a mat."
translations.idiomatic: Guo Pu gloss—ru as bedding.

sentences.zh: 」言身不自安。

translations.literal: It means the body cannot rest at ease.
translations.idiomatic: Figurative tossing—as if sleeping on thorns.

sentences.zh: 鎬，水名，在今長安縣西。

translations.literal: Hao is a river name west of Chang’an County today.
translations.idiomatic: Hao River west of Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 儷，偶也。

translations.literal: Li means paired; matched.
translations.idiomatic: Li: paired counterparts.

sentences.zh: 前書班固曰：「秦漢以來，山東出相，山西出將。

translations.literal: Ban Gu in the Former Han says: "Since Qin and Han times, east of the mountains has produced ministers, west of the mountains has produced generals.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Gu’s east-west divide of civil and martial talent.

sentences.zh: 」若白起、王翦、李廣、辛慶忌之流，皆山西人也。

translations.literal: Men such as Bai Qi, Wang Jian, Li Guang, and Xin Qingji—all were natives of the western hills.
translations.idiomatic: The western pantheon—Bai Qi to Xin Qingji.

sentences.zh: 潼，谷名。

translations.literal: Tong is a valley name.
translations.idiomatic: Tong Gorge as landmark.

sentences.zh: 谷有水，曰潼水，即潼關。

translations.literal: The valley holds a stream called Tong Water—that is Tong Pass.
translations.idiomatic: Tong River threading Tongguan.
